passages.passage_text: Independent testing of certified products is an essential feature of the Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS) Certification Marks Scheme. For this purpose, BIS has a network of laboratories in the country, which are in a position to issue around 4200 test reports a year. These laboratories are being constantly expanded and their testing facilities augmented and modernised. BIS has also recognised around 180 laboratories belonging to public and private sectors for testing products which are covered under various Indian Standard specifications.

One of the avowed goals of elementary education is improving learning achievement, apart from access and retention. This is a crucial area of concern for planners and educationists as investment in elementary education has not been given commensurate returns. The scenario with regard to learner achievement has been pretty bleak over the decades. Two national surveys of achievement in primary school children conducted by NCERT are a case in point. The survey on achievement in mathematics was conducted in 1965-66. The second on attainment in language, mathematics was conducted in 1990. Both surveys have indicated low levels of learner achievement.

Although studies have shown that India's performance in health (level of welfare of the poor) (UNDP, 1992), the health status, in general, has improved. Life expectancy and infant survival conditions are better than what is predicted at birth (LEB) increased from 36.7 years in 1951 and 1992, life expectancy to 10 per thousand and IMR fell from 139 per thousand five births in 1972 to 79 per thousand in 1992. But the improvement has not been even across regions and between genders.

An attempt is made here to look at the inter-regional disparities in health services. Performance in spread of health has no bearing on health status. Health survival conditions are not good in India. There is regional disparity in health improvement in India. Reports of UNDP are the only reliable reports in matters of health. India has surpassed many countries in terms of health improvement from 1951 to 1992.

While liberalisation essentially involves dismantling the barriers to entry and exit and the lifting of controls, it does not automatically imply deregulation of all markets. Regulation assumes its own level of sophistication in terms of ensuring that the system operate without unnecessarily restraining it, while at the same time protecting the financial system from its own excesses. In banking sector, for instance, while liberalisation can bring about a removal of barriers, it may accompany by banks and credit institutions, it is accompanied by a more stringent set of prudential, supervisory and regulatory structures. Similarly, in many other regulated markets, while liberalisation could mean the opening up of such markets to new products and new participants, regulation is put in place to protect consumers' interests and to prevent excessive speculation.

The Prime Minister's promise of a structural adjustment programme with a human face remains a pipedream. When the reforms started, he had assured once the fiscal imbalances were controlled. This assurance is yet to be realised. Recently, he said that 'only when the growth momentum picks up, the resources needed for these programmes can be mobilised.' Such indefinite postponement of reforms with a human face thus leaving out a major chunk of our society is in desperate need of social welfare programmes.
